main_sections.title: Canvas of Soil
main_sections.poem_text:
Palette of earth, rich and deep,
Where dreams of gardeners seep.
Brushstrokes of seeds, planted true,
Awaiting spring's vibrant hue.

Blossoms bloom, a painted sight,
Dancing in the morning light.
Shades of green, red, and blue,
Nature's artwork, ever new.

Each plot, a canvas wide,
Where art and life coincide.
In the hands of those who till,
Gardens become paintings still.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: This twelve-line poem by Maya Anthony draws an extended metaphor between gardening and painting. Earth becomes a painter's palette, seeds are brushstrokes, garden plots are canvases, and the gardener becomes an artist. The tone is appreciative and celebratory, marvelling at how nature and human effort together create beauty. The dominant imagery is visual — colours (green, red, blue), light (morning light), and the act of blooming — reinforcing the garden-as-painting conceit. The rhyme scheme is AABB throughout, lending a gentle, musical quality. The poem may be read as an allegory: the garden represents life's journey and growth, the diversity of colours symbolises harmony, and the tilling gardener stands for anyone who cultivates beauty through patient effort.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Appreciation + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Discuss what all you see in a garden. Think of the colours you see and where you see them. Share your responses with your teacher.

Look at the picture of a garden and a painting given below. Speak about any similarities between the garden and the painting using the sentence frames: 'Just as a garden ______, similarly, a painting ______'; 'A garden and a painting, both ______.'; '______ is common to both a garden and a painting.'; 'Like a garden, a painting too ______.'

Let us acquaint ourselves with the meanings of palette (thin oval/rectangular board for mixing colours), hue (shade of a colour), and canvas (here: painting). Now, look at the painting given and identify palette, canvas, and select a hue.

question_bank.stem: Identify the palette in the painting
Identify the canvas in the painting
Select a hue from the painting

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the poem again and complete the summary of each stanza by filling in the blanks. (1) The ______ is portrayed as a rich palette where gardeners' __ __ __ a __ s flourish in the form of ______, awaiting spring. (2) The garden flowers ______ into a beautiful display of different b __ __ __ __ __ m s, resembling a ______ by Mother Nature, in the light of morning. (3) Each garden is likened to a wide __ __ n __ __ s, integrating art and ______. Through the efforts of gardeners, gardens transform into still-life ______.

question_bank.stem: Stanza 1: The ______ is portrayed as a rich palette
Stanza 1: gardeners' __ __ __ a __ s flourish
Stanza 1: in the form of ______, awaiting spring
Stanza 2: garden flowers ______ into beautiful display
Stanza 2: resembling a ______ by Mother Nature
Stanza 3: likened to a wide __ __ n __ __ s
Stanza 3: integrating art and ______
Stanza 3: gardens transform into still-life ______

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Select the appropriate title for each stanza from those given below. There are two extra titles. 1. Nature's Work of Art  2. Sweet-smelling Blossoms  3. Gardens as Living Canvases  4. Earth and Possibilities  5. The Painter's Canvas

question_bank.stem: Select title for Stanza 1 from the 5 options
Select title for Stanza 2 from the 5 options
Select title for Stanza 3 from the 5 options

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the poetic devices in Column 1 to the examples in Column 2. Discuss your responses in pairs. 1. Imagery  2. Metaphor  3. Rhyme Scheme  4. Tone  5. Mood  6. Speaker  7. Alliteration → (i) appreciative (ii) AABB (iii) 'Blossoms bloom' (iv) colours, brushstrokes, blossoms, shades of green (v) a gardener (vi) garden as a painting, plot as canvas, seeds as brushstrokes (vii) joyful

question_bank.stem: Match: Imagery
Match: Metaphor
Match: Rhyme Scheme
Match: Tone
Match: Mood
Match: Speaker
Match: Alliteration

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts from the poem and answer the questions that follow. (Extract 1: 'Brushstrokes of seeds, planted true, / Awaiting spring's vibrant hue.'  Extract 2: 'Each plot, a canvas wide, / Where art and life coincide.')

question_bank.stem: Extract 1(i): The poet has used a metaphor in 'Brushstrokes of seeds'. Which option also uses a metaphor? A. Her mother's heart heard her heartfelt request with kindness. B. She has a heart of gold. C. Her heart did a dance of joy on seeing the new doll. D. She has a very kind heart.
Extract 1(ii): Complete the sentence. The phrase 'planted true' is significant because it implies ______.
Extract 1(iii): Why has the poet used the word 'hue' instead of 'colours' in the extract?
Extract 1(iv): Complete the analogy. Summer: hot :: Spring : ______

Extract 1(v): Assertion (A): Gardeners wait for Spring. Reason (R): Gardens are worth painting in Spring. Select the correctly suited option: A. Both true and R is correct explanation. B. Both true but R is not correct explanation. C. A true but R false. D. A false but R true.
Extract 2(i): What does 'Each plot' refer to in this extract?
Extract 2(ii): Select which option imitates the rhyme scheme. A. beautiful and clear / laughter and cheer  B. beautiful and clear / laughter and tears
Extract 2(iii): Select the line that conveys that gardening blends aesthetic beauty with natural growth.
Extract 2(iv): Complete the sentence. The plot is likened to a canvas suggesting that ______.
Extract 2(v): Why has the poet most likely used the word 'wide' instead of 'long' in 'canvas wide'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Give reasons for the comparisons made by the poet in the poem. 1. A painter is compared to a gardener because ______. 2. A palette is like earth as ______. 3. The brushstrokes are like seeds because ______. 4. A canvas is similar to a garden plot as ______.

question_bank.stem: Why is a painter compared to a gardener?
Why is a palette like earth?
Why are brushstrokes like seeds?
Why is a canvas similar to a garden plot?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions. 1. How does the metaphor 'Brushstrokes of seeds' enhance the understanding of gardening as an art form? 2. What can you infer about the poet's perspective on the relationship between nature and creativity from 'Each plot, a canvas wide, / Where art and life coincide'? 3. Do you think the imagery in the poem successfully paints a vivid picture in the reader's mind? 4. Support the view that the poet's mention of the colour yellow, besides red, blue and green, would have lent effectively to the imagery. 5. Considering 'Gardens become paintings still', what can you interpret about the poet's view on the timelessness of nature's beauty? 6. Justify the title 'Canvas of Soil'.

question_bank.stem: How does 'Brushstrokes of seeds' enhance understanding of gardening as art?
Infer the poet's perspective on nature and creativity from lines 'Each plot, a canvas wide, / Where art and life coincide.'
Does the imagery successfully paint a vivid picture?
Support the view that mention of yellow would have lent effectively to the imagery.
What does 'Gardens become paintings still' imply about the poet's view on timelessness of nature's beauty?
Justify the title 'Canvas of Soil'.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet refers to the shades of green, red, and blue in the poem. Read names of different shades of these colours (navy blue, indigo, cobalt blue, denim, sky blue, ice blue; pine green, India green, apple green, jade, olive, pistachio; rusty red, salmon, scarlet, crimson, blood red, vermilion). Discuss in pairs any two things you can associate with these colours.

You have studied painting-related words like palette, brushstrokes, shades, hue, colours, and canvas. Now read the paragraph in the art studio and discuss in pairs what the underlined painting-related words might mean (easels, tonal range, portrait, underpainting, mural). Discuss using the frame: 'I think ______ means ______ because the passage talks about ______.'

listening.transcript_text: A young girl describes her school garden to an audience. The garden has beautiful flowers in three rows: the first row has pink flowers, the second row has red roses, and the third row has white jasmines, all with butterflies fluttering around them. The garden also has several useful plants placed in pots in both left corners. The right corners have 20 potted evergreen plants. The garden is bordered with bricks that members of the Nature Club painted white and red. A grand banyan tree stands in the centre, also bordered with bricks in a circle. The Art and Craft Club members created items from waste materials — small scarecrows, pinwheels, and dustbins — and painted the garden bench near the entrance. The girl invites her audience to visit and spend time in the school garden.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a young girl describe her school garden. As you listen, identify which of the following 1–3 she does not talk about. (Images shown: 1. garden bench, 2. garden hose/tap, 3. butterfly)

question_bank.stem: Identify which of items 1–3 the girl does NOT talk about

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will once again listen to the young girl. As you listen, circle the correct answer: 1. The colour of flowers in the first row (white/red/pink). 2. The type of flowers in the second row (marigold/jasmine/rose). 3. Position of the useful plants (left corners/right corners/left and right corners). 4. The number of potted evergreen plants (15/20/25). 5. The paint colour on bricks bordering the garden (black and white/red and black/white and red). 6. Type of tree in the centre of the garden (neem/peepal/banyan). 7. Things created with waste material (dustbins/plant name boards/bird houses).

question_bank.stem: 1. The colour of flowers in the first row
2. The type of flowers in the second row
3. Position of the type of the useful plants
4. The number of potted evergreen plants
5. The paint colour on bricks bordering the garden
6. Type of tree in the centre of the garden
7. Things created with waste material

tasks_verbatim.task_text: People wish to have a garden at home. Some like a flower garden and some a vegetable garden. Think and note some advantages of both these types as gardens for homes.

Would you like to have a flower garden or a vegetable garden at home? Why? Take turns with your partner and speak your points aloud, one by one, using the given sentence prompts: 'I prefer ______ to ______ because...'; 'For me, it is a ______ instead of a ______ due to...'; 'If I had a choice I'd rather have a ______ than a ______ as...'; 'I would prefer ______ rather than ______ since...'

Write a descriptive piece of two to three paragraphs describing the details and colours in the garden you have visited. Focus on how different shades of blue, red, and green interact, create contrast, and bring the garden to life. Pay attention to details like the texture of petals, the varying greens of leaves, and the way light affects the colours.

Mini-Project: Explore the multifaceted world of gardens — their design, cultural significance, artistic inspiration, and the art of gardening itself. Duration: a few weeks. Select any TWO assignments (any one from 1 or 2, and any one from 3 or 4): (1) Research and collect information on any FIVE famous gardens of India (e.g. Amrit Udyan, Brindavan Gardens) — focus on history, design elements, and cultural significance; prepare a short report and make a presentation. (2) Explore various art forms (painting, sculpture) inspired by gardens; select an art piece and analyse how the garden is represented; create a visual or written response inspired by the garden. (3) Design your own garden on paper or using digital tools; incorporate elements and artistic inspirations from parts 1/2; present choices in design and artistic influences to the class. (4) Interview with a Gardener or Landscaping Expert — approach a local gardener or landscaping expert, prepare questions focused on understanding the practical aspects of gardening/landscaping, write a summary of your experience.

question_bank.stem: Assignment option 1: Research five famous gardens of India and make a presentation
Assignment option 2: Explore art forms inspired by gardens; create a visual or written response
Assignment option 3: Design your own garden on paper or digital tools
Assignment option 4: Interview a local gardener or landscaping expert; write a summary

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read and enjoy the poem 'A Sea of Foliage Girds Our Garden Round' by Toru Dutt — a sonnet describing the vibrant, colour-rich garden with tamarinds, mango trees, palms, red seemul flowers, bamboos, and a moonlit lotus transforming into a cup of silver. The poem evokes the experience of being overwhelmed ('swoon / Drunken with beauty') at nature's primeval beauty.
